Validated this end-to-end on my setup — **it works**. Summary: built FRR cleanly from a fresh git worktree off your branch.

**Setup**
- Fetched your `fix-worktree` branch, head `d134f4f65`
- Created a fresh worktree (`git worktree add`) at a non-neighboring location (`/data/sonic-buildimage-wt-test`, parent repo at `/data/sonic-buildimage`) — i.e. exactly the case #28007 didn't cover
- `make init` (52 submodule paths), `make configure PLATFORM=vs`, then built the FRR deb

**Result: ✅ success**
- `frr_10.5.4-sonic-0_amd64.deb` (5.98 MB) + `frr-dbgsym`, `frr-pythontools`, `frr-snmp`, `frr-snmp-dbgsym` all produced and valid (`dpkg-deb -I` → `Package: frr, Version: 10.5.4-sonic-0`)
- `SONIC_IMAGE_VERSION` resolved correctly to `<branch>.0-d134f4f65` (the pre-#28007 bug produced an empty `.0-` because in-container git was broken — that's gone)
- Zero git failures throughout the in-container build: `Error 128` = 0, `not a git repository` = 0, `Not inside a git worktree` = 0, no `stg` failures
- Confirmed the container ran with your mount: `-v /data/sonic-buildimage/.git:/data/sonic-buildimage/.git:rw -w /sonic`
- Host-side git stayed healthy at repo root and in submodules after the build

Your `git submodule foreach` fixup expands correctly through make (`$$`→`$`, command substitution runs in foreach's shell) and rewrites each submodule `.git` to the right absolute gitdir.

One small note for posterity, not a blocker: on my worktree the **top-level** `.git` was written as an absolute path by `git worktree add`, so it lined up with your absolute-path common-dir mount and in-container top-level git was fine. If someone hits a setup where the top-level `.git` is relative (older git, or a relocated worktree), in-container top-level git could resolve outside the mount — but `SONIC_IMAGE_VERSION` is computed host-side so it wouldn't break the version, and FRR (the git-heavy package) built fine here regardless. Worth keeping in mind if a future report shows top-level (non-submodule) in-container git issues.

> This is a review comment on the 202605 backport PR. The same concerns apply to the parent master PR #27863.

## Concern: Scope and approach of this fix

### 1. Platform-specific problem solved with a global workaround

The root cause here is a boot timing race on Cisco SmartSwitch (8102-28FH-DPU) — the `xt_addrtype` kernel module isn't loaded early enough, and at 9600 baud console speed the window is even wider. This is a platform-specific boot performance issue.

However, the fix modifies `docker.service.conf` which applies to **every SONiC platform** — not just SmartSwitch, not just Cisco. Rather than fixing the actual problem (module load ordering or the readiness check), this blanket-disables a Docker 28 capability for all of SONiC.

The previous PR #27341 had the right approach (readiness script), and issue #28030 proposed an improved version (`modprobe xt_addrtype` + addrtype match verification) that would fix the root cause without disabling the feature globally. This PR removes that script entirely instead of improving it.

Additionally, verification is limited to Cisco SmartSwitch only — there is no regression testing on standard T0/T1/T2 platforms, even though the change modifies Docker startup for all devices.

### 2. Disabling IPv6 iptables management has broader implications

SONiC already runs with `--iptables=false` (IPv4) — but that was a deliberate design choice from day one since SONiC manages its own firewall rules. Adding `--ip6tables=false` is not a design choice; it's a workaround for a boot timing bug on one platform.

With `--ip6tables=false`, Docker will not program IPv6 NAT, masquerade, or port-mapping rules for bridge-mode containers. SONiC has several containers that use Docker bridge networking:

- **`database`** containers on multi-ASIC platforms (`NET="bridge"`)
- **`dhcp_server`** — explicitly bridge mode, with iptables rules for syslog over docker0
- **`redfish`** — bridge mode with port mapping (`-p 443:18080`)

Today the impact may be low since these are primarily IPv4 workloads. But if SONiC ever needs IPv6 bridge networking (DHCPv6 relay, IPv6 management interfaces, IPv6 redfish access), it will silently not work — and someone will spend days debugging before discovering this flag was set as a SmartSwitch boot workaround. Docker's own documentation [warns](https://docs.docker.com/engine/network/packet-filtering-firewalls/#prevent-docker-from-manipulating-firewall-rules) that setting `ip6tables` to `false` "is not appropriate for most users" and "is likely to break container networking."

---

> @rameshraghupathy , do the PR #27477 needs a revert of changes with this PR?

@vvolam No, this PR does not require reverting #27477/#27341.

#27341 added docker-netfilter-ready.sh to wait for basic IPv6 iptables readiness before dockerd starts. That was the right first fix and should remain.

This PR addresses a different gap found in further testing. With Docker 28, dockerd also programs IPv6 iptables/conntrack rules during bridge initialization. We reproduced cases where the readiness script passed, but dockerd still failed later while programming IPv6 rules. So this PR restores the pre-Trixie/pre-Docker-28 behavior by explicitly disabling Docker IPv6 iptables programming with --ip6tables=false.

So the two changes are complementary:
- #27341: avoid starting Docker before basic netfilter readiness
- this PR: avoid Docker 28 IPv6 iptables programming path during startup

@vvolam Removed it.

> I thought #27341 resolved the startup issue. Why is this PR needed now?

@saiarcot895     #27341 fixed the initial readiness gap, but further testing showed it is not sufficient for this failure.

In the failing case, docker-netfilter-ready.sh reports success, but dockerd still fails later during bridge/network initialization while programming IPv6 iptables/conntrack rules. That means the basic readiness checks from #27341 pass, but Docker 28 still exercises additional IPv6 iptables paths that were not used in the previous Docker 24 behavior.

This PR is needed to restore the pre-upgrade behavior explicitly:
--ip6tables=false

That prevents Docker from programming IPv6 iptables rules during startup, which avoids the first-boot race and prevents the cascade into database/config-setup/interfaces-config failures.
See dockerd release notes about add iptables=false to restore the behavior before docker 27: https://docs.docker.com/engine/release-notes/27/#ipv6

In FRR 10.5.4, `bgp graceful-restart-disable` sets the global GR mode to `GLOBAL_DISABLE`, which clears both `PEER_FLAG_GRACEFUL_RESTART` and `PEER_FLAG_GRACEFUL_RESTART_HELPER` for all peers. This causes `bgp_peer_send_gr_capability()` to skip sending the GR capability in OPEN messages.

Since `bgp_peer_send_llgr_capability()` gates on `PEER_CAP_RESTART_ADV` being set (which requires GR capability to have been sent), the LLGR capability is also never advertised to peers under this configuration.

Could you clarify how LLGR helper behavior is activated for the intended peers? Is there a per-peer `neighbor <X> graceful-restart-helper` applied elsewhere (e.g., via bgpcfgd), or is this PR intended to work with a companion change?

This happens *before* `ycabled`/`sonic-utilities` tests run, causing the namespace conflict that shadows pip's protobuf.

See the detailed analysis here: https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/pull/27825#issuecomment-4682800914

You're right that the root issue is build sequencing — on master, ycabled tests happen to finish before p4lang-pi installs, so the namespace conflict never manifests. The build order isn't deterministic, so it's inherently flaky.

But that's exactly why the fix needs to target the **system path**, not the `.deb` staging dir. The staging dir cleanup (what this patch does) was already working — the packaged `.deb` doesn't contain `google/__init__.py`. The problem is what `dpkg-buildpackage` leaves behind in the **live system** `/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/google/` as a build side effect. That's what pollutes the namespace for any test that runs afterward.

This updated patch (`rm ... python3.*/site-packages/...`) still has two issues:
1. Debian trixie uses `dist-packages`, not `site-packages` — the glob won't match anything, and without `-f` the `rm` will fail and break the p4lang-pi build
2. It still only cleans `$(CURDIR)/debian/p4lang-pi/...` (the staging dir), not the actual system path

We've addressed this in #27825 by adding an `rm -f /usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/google/__init__.py` in `src/p4lang/Makefile` **after** `dpkg-buildpackage` completes. This cleans the system path regardless of build ordering, making the fix deterministic rather than relying on timing luck. CI is running now and looking good so far.

The VS build failure is a **google namespace package conflict**, not a test flake. Both `sonic_ycabled` and `sonic_utilities` wheels fail with:

```
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'google.protobuf'
```

...despite protobuf 5.29.6 being installed via pip at `/usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/` (the sonic-utilities build log even shows `Requirement already satisfied: protobuf ... (5.29.6)`).

**Root cause:** pip's protobuf uses [implicit namespace packages](https://peps.python.org/pep-0420/) (no `google/__init__.py`). When something creates a `google/__init__.py` under `/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/google/`, it turns that path into a regular package, and Python stops searching other `sys.path` entries for `google.protobuf`. The pip-installed protobuf at `/usr/local/lib/` becomes invisible.

I reproduced this locally on Debian trixie — pip protobuf works fine on its own, but the moment you `touch /usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/google/__init__.py`, imports break:

```
=== Before google/__init__.py ===
protobuf OK
=== After google/__init__.py ===
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'google.protobuf'
```

The `remove-init-file.patch` removes `google/__init__.py` from the **p4lang-pi .deb staging dir**, so the installed .deb shouldn't contain it. But the build timing reveals the real issue — on 202605 CI, `p4lang-pi` is **built and installed (09:54) before** `ycabled` tests run (10:08). On master, ycabled tests happen to finish **before** p4lang-pi installs, so the conflict never manifests.

Something during the p4lang-pi build or install (or another package) is creating `google/__init__.py` in the system dist-packages path. The `.deb` patch only protects the package contents, not side effects from the build process or other packages that share the `google/` namespace.

**Possible fixes:**
1. Add an explicit cleanup after p4lang-pi install in `src/p4lang/Makefile` or `slave.mk`:
   `rm -f /usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/google/__init__.py`
2. Add a `google-namespace-fixup` step in the Dockerfile after the pip install line
3. Investigate what exactly creates the stale `__init__.py` (could be `dpkg-buildpackage` side effects during the p4lang-pi build, or another deb like `libdashapi`)

Follow-up commit `f03d70f` replaces the surgical re-entry guard with the structurally correct fix.

**What changed**

The first commit (8137d5a) added an `inWrite` re-entry guard inside `db_addWriteCB` plus opportunistic drain sites. That stops the SIGSEGV, but the underlying race remains: `db_addWriteCB` still calls `redisAsyncHandleWrite()` synchronously, and the deferred-write retry depends on a slow-path `evt_poll_tick` drain (~1s worst case). It also carried a dead `writeDeferred` flag that nothing read.

The follow-up commit does what the original `db_addWriteCB` comment hinted at: register the redis fd's writable readiness with evbus, so writes are driven by the event loop instead of called synchronously from the addWrite hook.

- evbus gains an optional `EVWriteCB` and a per-socket `want_write` flag. `busRead()` builds a `writefds` set from sockets that have `want_write` set and dispatches their `writeCB` when `pselect()` reports them writable.
- mod_sonic registers `db_writeCB` at socket-add time and toggles `want_write` from the hiredis `addWrite`/`delWrite` hooks. The hooks themselves no longer call `redisAsyncHandleWrite()` at all - they just flip the readiness flag, so synchronous recursion from within the write path is structurally impossible.

Progress is bounded by pselect cycles instead of the 1s tick. The `writeDeferred` flag and the tick-drain safety net are removed.

**Patch 0009**

Patch 0009 (`HSP_SONIC_SNDBUF` debug knob) stays in the source tree but is commented out in `series`. It is the forensic harness used to validate this fix - reviewers can apply it manually and set `HSP_SONIC_SNDBUF=<bytes>` to reduce the redis socket send buffer for repro investigations. It is not applied by the default build.

**Validation**

- LD_PRELOAD `write_eagain_shim.so` + unfixed binary (master) - SIGSEGV (262K recursive frames). Validated in the first round on this PR.
- LD_PRELOAD `write_eagain_shim.so` + this fix - completes cleanly, exit 124 after the 30s timeout, no core, port discovery finishes, sflow enters waitConfig steady state.
- Normal sflow workload on sonic-vs T0 with 32 ports + cEOS converged peers: 32 ports discovered, no crash, BGP 4xARISTA01-04T1 stayed established (9h13m uptime preserved across hsflowd restart).
- Tried to reproduce organically on the 32-port vlab by faking up to 633 PORT_TABLE entries and shrinking SO_SNDBUF down to the kernel minimum - the recursion does not trigger on this testbed because local redis-server drains the unix socket faster than the burst can build backpressure. The LD_PRELOAD shim test (above) remains the controlled reproducer; #27728 is the field witness.

Should sonic-buildimage now use v2.1.26-1 instead of v2.1.25-6? This new release also includes a small change that would allow sonic-buildimage hsflowd patch 0007 to be removed as well:  in the new release the "crash signals" are only sigaction'd if "-C" is on the command line.  Since there is no "-C" on the command line used by SONiC, the patch 0007 is no longer required. So if we can move to v2.1.26-1 then the only remaining patch would be the one that adjusts the Debian package parameters.

Closing this PR because #27806 supersedes it on master.

I verified that #27806 upgrades `host-sflow` to upstream `v2.1.26-1`, and that upstream tag already contains this PR's redis write-recursion / high-port-count SIGSEGV fix via commit:

https://github.com/sflow/host-sflow/commit/41f10e6a2068ff15856e2bd1f1af2df1a506461b

The upstream code now drives redis writes through evbus writable readiness (`db_writeCB` + `EVSocketSetWantWrite`) instead of calling `redisAsyncHandleWrite()` synchronously from `db_addWriteCB()`, which is the same fix this PR carried as a SONiC downstream patch.

So the local downstream patch from this PR is no longer needed on master once #27806 is merged. If older release branches need the crash fix without taking the full hsflowd/sflowtool upgrade, this PR's patch can still be reused as the minimal backport-friendly fix.

Updated to use flat `SYSTEM_DEFAULTS` list entries as suggested:

- `SYSTEM_DEFAULTS|swss_zmq` with `status: enabled/disabled`
- `SYSTEM_DEFAULTS|async_rec` with `status: enabled/disabled`

Removed the `route_performance` container from YANG — these entries now use the existing `SYSTEM_DEFAULTS_LIST` pattern (same as `tunnel_qos_remap`, `dhcp_server`, etc.). Force-pushed with the updated implementation.

> @deepak-singhal0408 Why can't we have these options in constants.yml? These are knobs for how internally the components work and why should this be in user configuration. We can have this disabled by default and enable it during testing by updating constants.yml. Once things stablize we can change the constants file to enable this option

 @dgsudarshan thanks for your comment. Having runtime knob for these changes will be highly desirable actually for the following reasons..
 Note: This PR just refactors the existing knobs from DEVICE_METADATA->SYSTEMS_DEFAULT, and consolidates based on their purpose/
 
  1. Runtime kill switch — if ZMQ causes issues in production, operators can disable it immediately (hset SYSTEM_DEFAULTS|swss_zmq status disabled + config reload) without needing an image rebuild or manual file editing at scale.
  2. Device-role selectivity — in a datacenter fabric, high-scale spine/aggregation switches benefit from ZMQ, while low-scale leaf/ToR switches may not need it. CONFIG_DB lets the provisioning system push different settings per device role without building separate images.
  3. Canary rollout — we can enable on a subset of devices first and expand gradually, which is important for a feature that changes the orchagent↔syncd communication path.
  4. Consistency — synchronous_mode (closely related knob controlling the same communication path) is already in CONFIG_DB. Splitting related knobs across CONFIG_DB and constants.yml would be confusing.
 
Note that SYSTEM_DEFAULTS isn't operator-facing — there's no config CLI command to modify it. By default, these entries don't exist in CONFIG_DB (disabled), and the code treats missing entries as disabled. They're only added deliberately via provisioning or sonic-db-cli when a deployment is ready to enable them.

Fixed in ed5fedb14 (additive commit, signed off — no force-push/amend):

- **Enable** on `y|1|yes|true` — restores the previously documented `1|yes|true` tokens that the earlier `*)` branch was treating as "invalid → disabled." Existing `sonic_install.reboot=1` bootargs reboot again instead of silently dropping to the installer shell.
- **Disable** on `n|0|no|false` (symmetric opt-out aliases).
- **Unrecognized or empty** value → warn but keep the **default (reboot)**, not disable. This matches the "reboot on success unless explicitly disabled" model and closes the `sonic_install.reboot=` (empty-value) footgun you flagged — a typo is not an explicit disable.

Verified with `sh -n` plus a token table: `y/1/yes/true → reboot`, `n/0/no/false → stay in shell`, `maybe`/empty → default reboot + warning.

Docs reconciled with the code:
- Verify-step **#3** (`=1` → reboot, banner `enabled by sonic_install.reboot=1`) now matches as written.
- Verify-step **#4** and the section-4 Enable/Disable/Invalid bullets are updated — an invalid or empty value now warns and preserves the default reboot rather than disabling.

Non-blocking items acknowledged (no change): the missing `set -e` stays intentional (explicit rc-checking keeps `drop_shell` reachable), and the backport boxes stay unchecked (default-flip + banner = feature, not a backport candidate).

This PR broke marvell-prestera armhf build:

```
5.087 Package libgoogle-perftools4 is not available, but is referred to by another package.
5.087 This may mean that the package is missing, has been obsoleted, or
5.087 is only available from another source
5.087 However the following packages replace it:
5.087   libgoogle-perftools4t64
5.087 
5.091 E: Package 'libgoogle-perftools4' has no installation candidate
```

From the pipeline build for this PR:

```
[ building ] [ target/docker-fpm-frr.gz ] 
[ cached ] [ target/docker-fpm-frr.gz ]
```

Thanks for reporting this @saiarcot895.

**Root cause:** On Trixie armhf, the Debian time64 transition renames `libgoogle-perftools4` → `libgoogle-perftools4t64`. On amd64/arm64, the old name still works as a virtual/transitional package, but on armhf it's completely gone — only `libgoogle-perftools4t64` is available.

**Why CI didn't catch it:** The marvell-prestera-armhf build had a cache hit for `docker-fpm-frr.gz` (as shown in your logs: `[ cached ] [ target/docker-fpm-frr.gz ]`), so the Dockerfile was never actually rebuilt on that arch during CI.

**Planned fix:** Change `libgoogle-perftools4` → `libgoogle-perftools4t64` in `dockers/docker-fpm-frr/Dockerfile.j2`. Since docker-fpm-frr is always Trixie-based (`DOCKER_SWSS_LAYER_TRIXIE`), this should work across all archs — `sonic-slave-trixie/Dockerfile.j2` already uses `libgoogle-perftools4t64` without any arch conditional.

Do you have any other suggestions or concerns with this approach? I'll put up a fix PR shortly.

**`sonic-buffer-queue.yang` has the same missing leafref issue**

`VOQ_BUFFER_QUEUE_LIST.port` is a plain `string` with no leafref to PORT — structurally identical to the `VOQ_QUEUE_LIST.ifname` gap this PR fixes. The non-VOQ `BUFFER_QUEUE_LIST` already has a leafref, so GCU will have the same blind spot for buffer queue entries on VOQ chassis when a port is removed.

`sonic-system-port.yang` (`SYSTEM_PORT_LIST.ifname`) is in a similar situation, though arguably more nuanced since it holds entries for all linecards.

Would be worth applying the same union+must pattern to `sonic-buffer-queue.yang` as a follow-up.

Revised the fix per @-discussion: instead of gating SAI out of the bookworm pass (which broke `syncd` configure since gearbox dockers `gbsyncd-{broncos,milleniob,agera2}` depend on `$(SYNCD)`), `sai-xgs.mk` now uses **dual versions**:

- `BLDENV=trixie` → SAI **15.2.0_GA** (this is what the final image consumes; `IMAGE_DISTRO=trixie`)
- `BLDENV=bookworm` → SAI **14.3.0_GA** (transitional, satisfies syncd link-time `-lsai` for the gearbox container syncd binary)

The bookworm syncd binary is only baked into `gbsyncd-*` containers, which dynamically resolve `-lsai` against `libsai{broncos,milleniob,agera2}` at runtime — so the link-time SAI version is functionally irrelevant. This will be removed once broadcom is fully migrated to trixie.

> Team, Please check my comments below
> 
> High — Bugs to Fix Before Merge
> 
> 1. platform_device_register_resndata — wrong error check (pddf_multifpgapci_i2c_xiic_module.c)
> 
> platform_device_register_resndata() returns ERR_PTR(errno) on failure, never NULL. The guard if (!platform_device) only catches NULL and would store a poison pointer into i2c_xiic_privdata->platform_devices[index], triggering a crash on the next platform_device_unregister call.
> 
> // BUG — does not catch ERR_PTR returns struct platform_device *platform_device = platform_device_register_resndata(...); if (!platform_device) { ret = -ENOMEM; // also wrong — should use PTR_ERR(platform_device) goto free_clk_lookup; } Should be:
> 
> struct platform_device *platform_device = platform_device_register_resndata(...); if (IS_ERR(platform_device)) { ret = PTR_ERR(platform_device); goto free_clk_lookup; }
> 
> 2. pddf_multifpgapci_remove — pci_irq_vector() called beyond allocated count (pddf_multifpgapci_driver.c)
> 
> The teardown loop iterates MAX_NUM_MSI_VECTORS (3) unconditionally, but pci_irq_vector(dev, i) will BUG/WARN if i >= num_msi_vectors (the MSI-X/MSI table only has as many entries as were allocated):
> 
> // BUG — i can exceed pci_privdata->num_msi_vectors for (unsigned i = 0; i < MAX_NUM_MSI_VECTORS; ++i) { if (pci_privdata->msi_domain_irq_chip_data[i]) regmap_del_irq_chip(pci_irq_vector(dev, i), // ← fault if i >= num_msi_vectors pci_privdata->msi_domain_irq_chip_data[i]); } Fix: gate on pci_privdata->num_msi_vectors:
> 
> for (unsigned i = 0; i < pci_privdata->num_msi_vectors; ++i) { if (pci_privdata->msi_domain_irq_chip_data[i]) regmap_del_irq_chip(pci_irq_vector(dev, i), pci_privdata->msi_domain_irq_chip_data[i]); } for (unsigned i = 0; i < MAX_NUM_MSI_VECTORS; ++i) kfree(pci_privdata->regmap_irqs[i]);
> 
> 3. Clock unregistered before child platform devices are torn down (pddf_multifpgapci_driver.c)
> 
> In pddf_multifpgapci_remove, clk_hw_unregister_fixed_rate is called, but the xiic platform devices registered by pddf_multifpgapci_i2c_xiic_module are only freed when that module is unloaded (via pddf_multifpgapci_i2c_xiic_detach). If the PCI driver is removed while the xiic module is still loaded, the xiic platform devices will still hold a reference to the now-freed clk_hw, causing a use-after-free when the xiic driver accesses the clock.
> 
> The fix is to ensure the protocol module detach callbacks (pddf_multifpgapci_i2c_xiic_detach) are invoked before the clock is freed in pddf_multifpgapci_remove. Check whether the existing delete_fpga_data_node path triggers detach, or add an explicit ordered teardown.
> 
> Medium — Should Fix 4. irq_line_mask type mismatch in dev_operation (pddf_multifpgapci_driver.c)
> 
> register_msi_domain takes unsigned long long irq_line_mask, but in dev_operation it is read with sscanf(..., "%x", &irq_line_mask) where irq_line_mask is unsigned int. The %x conversion into unsigned int then widened to unsigned long long on the call. For 32-bit masks this works accidentally, but upper bits are silently dropped for wider masks and the types don't match:
> 
> // dev_operation: unsigned int msi_domain, irq_line_mask, unmask_reg, status_reg; // unsigned int int sscanf_result = sscanf(buf, "register_msi_domain %u %31s %x %x %x", &msi_domain, irq_chip_name, &irq_line_mask, ...); // then passed to: register_msi_domain(..., (unsigned long long)irq_line_mask); // implicit upcast Either change the signature to unsigned int irq_line_mask (consistent with 32-bit max reg_width), or use unsigned long long + %llx in sscanf.
> 
> 5. should_fpga_use_msi — KeyError if dev_attr is absent (pddfparse.py)
> 
> def should_fpga_use_msi(dev) -> bool: if "use_msi" in dev: check_fpga_version_cmd = dev["dev_attr"].get("check_fpga_version_cmd", "") If dev_attr is missing from the JSON, this raises KeyError instead of gracefully returning False. Fix:
> 
> check_fpga_version_cmd = dev.get("dev_attr", {}).get("check_fpga_version_cmd", "") 6. input_clk_hz == 0 — silent misconfiguration when not specified (pddf_multifpgapci_driver.c)
> 
> If input_clk_hz is omitted from the PDDF JSON, pddfparse.py defaults it to 0. A clk_hw_register_fixed_rate with 0 Hz succeeds, but the downstream xiic driver will derive wrong timing and likely hang or mis-program the SCL divisor. At minimum add a warning; if MSI is enabled and clk_hz == 0, fail loudly:
> 
> if (n_msi_vectors > 0 && clk_hz == 0) { printk(KERN_ERR "%s: [%s] input_clk_hz must be > 0 for MSI path\n", MULTIFPGA, **FUNCTION**); ret = -EINVAL; goto free_bars; }

The issue to deal with here has not been seen on 202505. It was noticed on earlier release, but due to the watchdog triggered and no storage device ready, there is not much information to look into about why the storage device could not show up.

Given this change, the ambiguity in the argument names will become a significant usability issue:

1. --vrf is too generic and does not indicate that it applies specifically to ZMQ.
2. --gnmi_vrf is inconsistent with the naming convention used for --vrf.
3. Operators will have no clear way to distinguish which VRF applies to which interface.
4. The comment in gnmi-native.sh — "Add VRF parameter when mgmt-vrf enabled" — does not clarify the usage.

Recommendation:
The PR should rename the arguments to improve clarity and consistency:

- Replace --vrf with --zmq_vrf
- Either keep --gnmi_vrf or rename it to --nbi_vrf for better alignment and clarity

This is a usability issue that is likely to cause operational confusion if not addressed.

@kwangsuk @hdwhdw 
The reason to *keep* the `--vrf` option for ZMQ was to avoid any breakage in tooling scripts that current operators might already have in their environments, as this isn't a new argument that we are adding.
I think it is worth mentioning that in https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-gnmi/blob/master/gnmi_server/server.go#L227 and https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-gnmi/blob/master/telemetry/telemetry.go#L192 as well we have the same matching arguments representing the VRF for ZMQ and for GNMI.

I can rename `--gnmi_vrf` to `--nbi_vrf`, and `--vrf` to `--zmq_vrf`, but I just want to make sure we are on the same page before making the change.

cc: @anders-nexthop @bhouse-nexthop

> LGTM:
> 
> * I think gnmi_vrf is quite clear already. nbi_vrf doesn't add enough value and sounds a bit internal jargon-ish.
> * vrf->zmq_vrf only adds value when operator does `docker ps`. If they are just using `CONFIG_DB` this doesn't add much.
> * We should land this PR. flags are mostly internal-ish and not really operator facing. The CONFIG_DB schema is pretty clear which is what matters.

Thanks for the clarification.
I still think the current argument names introduce some ambiguity (especially --vrf vs --gnmi_vrf), and renaming to something like --zmq_vrf would improve clarity for operators. But I agree this is not a blocking issue given that these flags are mostly internal and the CONFIG_DB schema is the primary operator interface.
We can always revisit naming if this becomes a usability concern later.
Approving.
